coredata.dc:title: Folic acid-modified photoluminescent dialdehyde carboxymethyl cellulose crosslinked bionanogels for pH-controlled and tumor-targeted co-drug delivery
coredata.dc:creator: Pooresmaeil, Malihe
Namazi, Hassan
coredata.dc:description: This work aimed to fabricate a new photoluminescent bionanogel with both targeted anticancer drug delivery and bioimaging potentials. Briefly, at first photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized from the low-cost and more available black pepper with traditional medicinal properties. The as-synthesized dialdehyde carboxymethyl cellulose (DCMC) was used as a safe crosslinker for gelatin crosslinking in the presence of CDs (CDs/DCMC-Gel). Eventually, the residual amine functional groups of gelatin were used for the conjugation of CDs/DCMC-Gel with folic acid (FA) ((CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels). All employed physicochemical characterization methods approved the (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels fabrication route. SEM analysis specified the spherical morphology with a diameter of ~70–90 nm for it. Curcumin (CUR) and doxorubicin (DOX) respectively were loaded with drug entrapment efficiency of about 44.0% and 41.4%. The release rate for both drugs in acidic conditions was higher than in physiological conditions. In vitro antitumor experiments; MTT, DAPI staining, cellular uptake, and cell cycle tests showed the superior anticancer effect of the CUR@DOX@(CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA in comparison with free CUR@DOX. Moreover, the (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA acted as a hopeful bio-imaging tool. Taken together, the designed (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA could be proposed as a promising nanosystem for efficient chemotherapy.

head.ce:title: Folic acid-modified photoluminescent dialdehyde carboxymethyl cellulose crosslinked bionanogels for pH-controlled and tumor-targeted co-drug delivery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology (RCPN), Tabriz University of Medical Science, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center for Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology (RCPN), Tabriz University of Medical Science, Tabriz, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: P.O. Box 51666, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work aimed to fabricate a new photoluminescent bionanogel with both targeted anticancer drug delivery and bioimaging potentials. Briefly, at first photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized from the low-cost and more available black pepper with traditional medicinal properties. The as-synthesized dialdehyde carboxymethyl cellulose (DCMC) was used as a safe crosslinker for gelatin crosslinking in the presence of CDs (CDs/DCMC-Gel). Eventually, the residual amine functional groups of gelatin were used for the conjugation of CDs/DCMC-Gel with folic acid (FA) ((CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels). All employed physicochemical characterization methods approved the (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels fabrication route. SEM analysis specified the spherical morphology with a diameter of ~70–90 nm for it. Curcumin (CUR) and doxorubicin (DOX) respectively were loaded with drug entrapment efficiency of about 44.0% and 41.4%. The release rate for both drugs in acidic conditions was higher than in physiological conditions. In vitro antitumor experiments; MTT, DAPI staining, cellular uptake, and cell cycle tests showed the superior anticancer effect of the CUR@DOX@(CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA in comparison with free CUR@DOX. Moreover, the (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA acted as a hopeful bio-imaging tool. Taken together, the designed (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA could be proposed as a promising nanosystem for efficient chemotherapy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Breast cancer with a high mortality rate across the globe is one of the biggest threats to women's healthy [1,2]. Chemotherapy is a usually used method for malignant tumors treatment. However, the used chemotherapeutic drugs like DOX frequently lead to unavoidable side effects and system cytotoxicities [3–8]. By considering these, the development of a new advanced therapeutic system is a crucial area in the biomedical field [9–11]. Today's environment-sensitive drug delivery systems, especially the drug delivery systems based on nanomaterials have been approved for their massive benefits in improving chemotherapeutic drug efficiency [12,13]. Until now, several drug delivery systems including polymers [14–18], nanoparticles (NPs) [18–21], dendrimers [22–24], microspheres [22], hydrogels [15,25,26], hydrogel nanocomposites [27], cyclodextrin based systems [28,29], and nanogels [30,31] have been displayed their significant efficacy in anticancer drug delivery applications.

The results of the preclinical and clinical studies have demonstrated that among these, the stimuli-responsive nanogels as an example of the nano-based drug delivery systems are more favorable because they can enhance the tumor-specific drug delivery and improve circulation stability in the blood [3,32]. Nanogels are three-dimensional, nanometric-sized hydrogels that combine the features of both NPs and hydrogels [33,34]. These nanomaterials can swell in water and allow spontaneous drug molecule loading [35]. Up to now, several types of polymers have been investigated for the fabrication of nanogels. Among them, gelatin is one of the more preferred owing to its excellent biocompatibility, low cost, non-immunogenicity, hydrophilic nature, biodegradability, and sufficient functional groups which make it a useful candidate for attaching the targeted ligands [36–38]. These nanogels; biopolymer-based nanogels are defined as bionanogels [15,39]. There are different ways for the fabrication of gelatin-based nanogels but the crosslinked nangels that contain the CN (Schiff's base) linkages are more promising in tumor therapy because these bonds are unstable under an acidic medium and lead to pH-responsive drug release behavior [40]. Glutaraldehyde and formaldehyde are two commonly used chemical crosslinkers for gelatin crosslinking. But, due to their possible toxicity [41] in recent decades, the naturally-derived crosslinkers with acceptable biocompatibility and biodegradability have been received increasing attention. In particular, DCMC is a low-cost, water-soluble, biocompatible, renewable, and abundant polysaccharide-based crosslinker that can obtain the chemically crosslinked gelatin contain CN linkages [42,43]. Thank these, in the current work the DCMC was selected for the gelatin crosslinking.

It is approved that the addition of nanosize engineering into a drug carrier formulation is the most promising way that can improve its efficiency [44]. CDs are emerging as promising materials for both drug delivery and cellular imaging owing to their high intrinsic fluorescence property, environmental friendliness, good water dispersibility, large surface area, and the presence of a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups on their structure [9,45–49]. The minimum size of CDs provides the possibility of their in vivo injections, as well as they can be used as probes in biological [50]. By considering these the study of the CDs biodegradability in living systems is vital to accelerate the translation to clinical innovations. A previously published study in 2019 showed that the CDs can biodegrade through the progressive loss of the structure in the presence of the human myeloperoxidase and eosinophil peroxidase; enzymes that respectively are expressed in activated neutrophils and eosinophil granulocytes [51].

On the other hand, the conjugation of each anticancer drug carrier with targeting ligands is another way to enhance the cell-specific and also intracellular delivery [52–54]. From the therapeutic methodology, numerous potential targeting approaches have been used which among them the FA is one of the well-believed cancer-targeting agents owing to its easy modification and low price [55].

Up to now, several studies were made to investigate the capability of the gelatin-based nanogels for cancer therapy. However, to the best of our knowledge, there is no detailed published investigation on the fabrication and evaluation of (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels for both bioimaging and CUR@DOX co-delivery. In fact, the main aim of this work is to introduce the CDs and FA in gelatin-based bionanogels structure to improve its efficiency. The synthesis process was performed in three steps. Initially, the fluorescent CDs were synthesized through the hydrothermal treatment method using black pepper as a raw material. Afterward, the mixture of gelatin and CDs were chemically crosslinked with DCMC. Eventually, the fabricated CDs/DCMC-Gel were conjugated with FA to achieve a targeted delivery drug system for breast cancer treatment. The fabricated system was described by several characterization techniques. Then the obtained (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionangels were assessed from the CUR and DOX co-delivery capability. Furthermore, it was studied for in vitro bioimaging, biocompatibility, and biodegradability. (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels displayed a high efficiency in both DOX and CUR loading and their controlled release. The in vitro cellular tests were also approved the biocompatibility of (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA. Consequently, the findings showed the great potential of pH-sensitive (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels for efficient tumor therapy.

Gelatin, sodium periodate (NaIO4), hydrochloric acid (HCl), ethanol (EtOH), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), cyclohexane, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), methanol, and sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) were purchased from Merck (Germany). The black pepper was obtained from the local store and washed with bidistilled water for further use. CMC with a viscosity of 15,000 mPa·s (1% in H2O, 25 °C) was supplied by Nippon Paper Chemicals Co., Ltd. Degree of substitution for the used CMC was about 0.55–1.0. Hydroxylamine hydrochloride, Span 20, borax, trinitrobenzenesulfonic acid (TNBS), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3), FA, N,N′-Dicyclohexylcarbodiimid (DCC), 4-(Dimethylamino)pyridine (DMAP), 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH), 3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazole-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT), paraformaldehyde, Triton X-100, CUR, and 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) were provided from Sigma-Aldrich. Bidistilled water was used in the preparation of all aqueous solutions. Fetal bovine serum albumin (FBS), penicillin-streptomycin, trypsin, ribonuclease (RnaseA), and all of the other utilized biological materials were attained from GIBCO, USA. DOX was prepared by Sobhan Pharmaceuticals Co. (Iran). A dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off 12,000 Da) was the product from Sigma-Aldrich. The MDA-MB-231 and HUVEC cells were supplied by the Iranian National Cell Bank (Pasteur Institute, Tehran, Iran).

ESCANMIRA3 scanning electron microscope (SEM) operating at an accelerating voltage of 5 kV was employed to explore the morphology and size of the fabricated samples, as well as, to perform the energy-dispersive X-ray spectra (EDX) analysis. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) instrument (Bruker Instruments (model Aquinox 55, Germany) was used to record the FT-IR spectra as KBr pellets with a resolution of 4cm−1 in 400–4000cm−1. The Ultraviolet-Visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were specified employing Shimadzu 1700 UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were acquired using a Jasco FP-8300 spectrofluorimeter (Japan) with a 1.0 cm quartz cell. The zeta potentials and hydrodynamic size were determined by dynamic light scattering (DLS-ZP/Particle Sizer (Microtrac, model Nanotrac Wave)). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, LEO 906E transmission electron microscope) was administered to observe the internal morphology. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra of the samples were subjected to characterize their crystalline features employing a Siemens diffractometer (wavelength 1.5406 Å). If required the samples were lyophilized with a laboratory freeze dryer (FD-8505/FD-5005-BT). The pH value of the used solutions was adjusted by a PHS-3C pH meter in desired pH value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots from black pepper
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were synthesized via the hydrothermal treatment method based on the use of natural precursors to obtain highly water-soluble CDs [56,57]. Typically, about 4 g of the black pepper was poured into a reaction vessel containing H2O (40 mL). The mixture was magnetically stirred and then sonicated respectively for 24 h and 30 min at room temperature. The mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated for 5 h at 180 °C. After cooling the resultant black carbonized solution to room temperature, it was centrifuged to remove the produced large particles. The brownish-yellow filtrate solution was dialyzed for 6 h with a dialysis bag, the dialysis water was exchanged every 30 min. Finally, the purified CDs were dried under reduced pressure and stored in a dark glass vial for the following use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of DCMC as a safe crosslinker
ce:section.ce:para: DCMC was prepared based on the previously published works with some modifications [58–60]. In a typical procedure, sodium periodate (1.225 g) and CMC (2.5 g) were dissolved in bidistilled water (75 mL). After complete dissolving, the pH of the solution was set at 3.5 with dilute HCl (1 M). The reaction was continued for 6 h at 40 °C in a tinfoil-wrapped vessel. The produced DCMC was precipitated by pouring the solution into a large amount of ethanol. The formed precipitate was collected by centrifugation. DCMC was interactively cross-washed with water and EtOH to eliminate the unreacted raw materials. Eventually, the produced pure DCMC was freeze-dried and then stored for further usages.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of aldehyde functional group content (AC)
ce:section.ce:para:
The aldehyde functional group content of the DCMC was determined by a simple titration process according to the previously reported procedure [58]. Briefly, about 0.3 g of DCMC was dissolved in 15 mL of bidistilled water. pH was adjusted to 5.0 with NaOH. The solution was treated with hydroxylamine hydrochloride (20 mL, 0.05 g/mL, pH 5.0) under agitation for 4 h at 40 °C. In the following, the liberated HCl was titrated by NaOH (1 M) and the consumed volume of the NaOH was recorded. The pristine CMC with equal concentration was titrated in the same procedure. In the end, the AC of DCMC was measured by the following formula:
(1)
AC
%
=
C NaOH
Vd − Vc
m / 242.16
× 100
where m, Vc, and Vd respectively are the dry mass of DCMC in g, the consumed volumes of NaOH for CMC and DCMC. Moreover, in this equation, the molecular weight of the repeating unit in DCMC is 242.16. The determination of the AC group content was performed three times and the reported results are the average of them.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of CDs containing DCMC-gelatin bionanogels (CDs/DCMC-gel)
ce:section.ce:para: Photoluminescent gelatin-based bionanogels were fabricated through an inverse miniemulsion method as described previously with slight modifications [61]. In this regard, initially, the mixture containing 2 mL of gelatin (10% w/v, solution in bidistilled water), CDs (0.04 g), and 2 mL of DCMC (10% w/v, solution in 0.1 M borax) was prepared as an aqueous phase. On the other hand, the oil phase was prepared via dissolving of Span 20 (0.2 g) in 10 mL of cyclohexane. In the following, the aqueous phase was added into the oil phase under sonication over a period of 5 min. In the following, the dropwise addition of the organic and aqueous phase mixture (nanogel emulsion) into acetone obtained the bionanogels; over this time the mixture was agitated employing a magnetic stirrer. The solid product was collected through centrifugation, purified via three times washing with water, and then dried under reduced pressure. The neat DCMC-Gel bionanogels were also prepared in the same route as well as the CDs/DCMC-Gel preparation, except that the aqueous phase was free from any CDs.

The degree of cross-linking of CDs/DCMC-Gel was estimated by TNBS assay; based on the amino reaction between the free groups of gelatin with TNBS. Briefly, at first, the 20 mg of each gelatin and CDs/DCMC-Gel bionanogels separately were mixed with 1 mL of NaHCO3 solution (4%) and 1 mL of TNBS solution (0.05%). The temperature of the mixture was adjusted at 60 °C and heated for 4 h at this temperature. In the following, the 3 mL of HCl (6 M) was added to the above mixture. The mixture was heated for another 1.5 h at 40 °C. In the end, the absorbance was obtained at 334 nm employing the UV–Vis spectrophotometer and Eq. (2).
(2)

The obtained degree of cross-linking of about 84.2% exhibit the successful cross-linking through the reduction in the free amino group content.

For the functionalization of CDs/DCMC-Gel with FA, initially, the FA (0.1 mmol) was activated with DCC (0.1 mmol) and DMAP (0.033 mmol) through the stirring in dry DMSO for 30 min. Thereafter, the FA solution was added to the dispersed CDs/DCMC-Gel bionanogel (120 mg) in dry DMSO. The reaction was continued for 6 h at room temperature. The resultant was washed with DMSO, water, and ethyl acetate. For further purification, the product was redispersed in acetone overnight, washed with water, and then dried in a vacuum oven [62].

The swelling behavior of the fabricated (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels was analyzed via gravimetric method after immersing at two different buffer solutions; pH 7.4 and pH 5.0 to characterize its pH-responsive behavior. Typically, at first, about 100 mg of sample was immersed in 10 mL of a buffer solution with well-known weight and pH values. The suspension was shaken for 24 h. After this time the mixture was centrifuged and the weight of the absorbed buffer by the samples was obtained based on the initial weight of the used buffer and also the weight of the supernatant. Finally, the swelling percentage is calculated as Eq. 3. [63]. The swelling test was performed in triplicate and the reported results are their average.
(3)
Swelling

Wi

× 100
In Eq. (1) the Ws, Wi,w, and Wsup,w respectively are the weight of dry samples, the weight of used buffer solution to prepare the suspension, and the weight of the supernatant.

Commonly biomedical applications of each newly synthesized system are affected by its colloidal stability. In this regard, the in vitro colloidal stability of (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels was studied after its dispersion in four different media; water, phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), FBS, and RPMI containing 10% (V/V) FBS. Then each dispersion was shaken at 37 °C for three days. Eventually, the stability was investigated through visual observation of the possible aggregations or changes in turbidity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro biodegradation of (CDs/DCMC-gel)-FA bionanogels in presence of trypsin
ce:section.ce:para:
In a typical procedure for exploring the enzymatic biodegradation behavior of the fabricated bionanogels, about 50 mg of (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels were immersed in a centrifuge tube containing 10 mL of PBS (pH 7.4); for each selected time separate tubes were used. When the equilibrium swelling was reached the PBS was drained and trypsin solution (4 mL, 50 μg/mL) was replaced. The set was shaken on a shaker incubator at 37 °C. At each predetermined time, the enzyme solution was discharged, the bionanogels were rinsed with deionized water and next dehydrated with ethanol. Eventually, the degraded (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels were dried under reduced pressure. The whole weight of centrifuge tubes and nanogels assigned as Wt. In the end, the Eq. 4 obtained the remaining weight percentage.
(4)
Remaining weight
%
=
Wt
W 0
× 100

In the above equation, the W0 and Wt respectively signify the original weight of each tube containing bionanogels and the remaining weight of each tube containing degraded bionanogels after each selected time. In vitro degradation test was done in triplicate and their averaged were presented.

The in-vitro DPPH assay was conducted to determine the antioxidant activity of (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels. Initially, the stock solution of DPPH was prepared in 40 μg/mL. Then each of the sample dispersion (3 mg in 2 mL) was immersed into a methanolic solution of DPPH (2 mL, 40 μg/mL). The mixture was incubated in a dark condition at room temperature. After 30 min, the absorbance of each solution was determined at 280–780 nm employing a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. Higher free radical scavenging activity is associated with a lower absorbance of the reaction mixture. DPPH test was performed in triplicate. Eventually, the antioxidant capacity; free-radical scavenging activity of the samples was measured consistent with the following equation:

Where A0 and A1 respectively represent the absorbance of DPPH solution in the same medium (2 mL methanol and 2 mL stock solution of DPPH) and supernatant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro co-drug load and release study
ce:section.ce:para:
Both CUR and DOX were co-loaded in (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels to obtain the system with enhanced anticancer activity. Firstly, (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels (30 mg) were dispersed in the ethanolic solution of CUR (500 ppm). The mixture was covered with aluminum foil and then the was shaken at room temperature overnight. To remove free CUR and collect the CUR-loaded (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels, the dispersion was collected by centrifugation and washed three times with ethanol. The collected supernatant after centrifugation and washing was examined employing UV–Vis spectroscopy and CUR standard calibration curve to obtain the amount of the un-loaded CUR. To continue the drug loading process, the CUR@(CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels were dispersed in PBS solution containing DOX (500 mg/mL). The vial was covered with aluminum foil and then the mixture was shaken at room temperature for 24 h. To remove the free DOX and collect the DOX-loaded CUR@(CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels, the dispersion was collected by centrifugation and then washed three times with PBS. The collected supernatant after centrifugation and washing was examined employing UV–Vis spectroscopy and DOX standard calibration curve to obtain the amount of un-loaded DOX. Finally, the drug loading capacity and entrapment efficiency were calculated according to the following equations:
(6)

% w

The time and the pH-dependent drug-release test were conducted at 37 °C in two different pHs; pH 5.0 and pH 7.4 containing SDS. Generally, 3 mL of DOX@CUR@(CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels suspension were transferred in dialysis bags (MWCO 12,000). The dialysis bags were then placed in 7 mL of the same buffer solution. The tubes were covered by aluminum foil and the mixture was shaken at 37 °C. For each release measurement time, 3 mL of the solution was sampled and the same amount of the fresh buffer was replaced. The released drug concertation was determined via determination of the separated solution adsorption using UV–Vis spectrometer, calibration curve, and Eq. (8). Both drug load and release experiments were repeated triplicate.
(8)

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro drug release kinetic studies
ce:section.ce:para:
The mathematical kinetic models are one important tool for the determination of the drug release mechanism from each carrier. Thanks to this, four usually studied drug release kinetic models; zero-order, first-order, Higuchi, and Korsmeyer-Peppas models (Eqs. (9)–(12)) were fitted with the obtained in vitro experimental drug release data to evaluate the mechanism of the co-drug releases [64,65]. The linear form of the studied models is introduced in the following.
(9)
Q =
k 0
t
(10)
LnQ = Ln
Q 0

k 1

(11)
Q =
k H

1 2
(12)
LnQ = nLnt + Ln
k p

In Eqs. (9)–(12) the k0, k1, kH, kP, factors respectively are rate constants of the zero-order, first-order, Higuchi, and Korsmeyer-Peppas. Moreover, the Q is the released drug percentage at time t. The “n” parameter of the Korsmeyer-Peppas model is the diffusion exponent and specify the accordance of the experimental results with each mechanism; “n” value of 1.0, 0.45>, and 0.45–1.0 respectively is obtained when the mechanism of release respectively is in case II transport, Fickian diffusion, and non-Fickian.
For all of the cellular tests, the untreated cells were defined as a control group.

In vitro cytotoxicity of the (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA, DOX@CUR, and DOX@CUR@(CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels was assessed toward the human breast cancer cell line employing the MTT assay [47]. Typically, the MDA-MB 231 cell line was seeded into 96-well plates and afterward incubated at 37 °C in atmospheric humidity (5% CO2, 95% air) to attach the bottom of the wells. After overnight, the old complete medium was drained and the cells were treated with the samples. The treated cells were incubated for another 48 h at 37 °C. In the following, the unconsumed medium was eliminated and 200 μL of MTT solution (0.5 mg/mL) was added to each well. The cells were incubated for 4 h at 37 °C. The residual MTT solution was discharged and the formed formazan crystals were dissolved as a result of the DMSO (200 μL) addition. Finally, the cell viability was determined via obtaining the absorbance of the solution at 570 nm employing the ELISA plate reader. The cell viability assay was also performed for (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels toward normal breast cells (MCF 10A) to more asses its biocompatibility. All MTT test results were presented as average ± SD (n = 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the cell internalization capability for the DOX@CUR and DOX@CUR@(CDs/DCMC-gel)-FA with fluorescent microscopy
ce:section.ce:para: According to the reported procedures [36,66] and with some changes the cell uptake test was conducted employing fluorescence microscopy imaging (qualitative) to explore the cell internalization potential of the DOX@CUR and DOX@CUR@(CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA. Typically, for the fluorescence microscopy imaging, MDA-MB 231 cells were seeded onto coverslips in the 6-well cell culture plate. After cells attachment, the cells were treated with the samples and then incubated for 3 h. In the end, the cells were washed with PBS solution and being observed by fluorescence microscopy (Olympus microscope Bh2-RFCA, Japan).

The morphology of the nuclei after treatment with the (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA, DOX@CUR, and DOX@CUR@(CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA was explored by fluorescent microscopy morphology to check the status of the nuclei from the apoptotic, necrotic, and healthy viewpoints. At first, the MDA-MB 231 cells were seeded in the 6-well plates having sterile coverslips and then incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. In the next step, the cells were treated with the samples and incubated for another 48 h. The cells were washed with PBS and fixed with paraformaldehyde (3 wt%). After 10 min the additional paraformaldehyde was removed and the cells were washed with fresh PBS. The fixed cells were permeabilized with Triton X-100 for 15 min. Then the cells were stained with DAPI for 5 min after washing with PBS. Eventually, the status of the nucleus was distinguished administring the fluorescence microscope (Olympus microscope Bh2-RFCA, Japan) [53].

According to [4] the cell cycle assay was conducted through the FACS Calibur flow cytometer to evaluate the cell's population frequencies in each phase of the cell cycle after treatment with (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels. In a typical procedure, MDA-MB 231 cells were seeded in a 6-well culture plate and then incubated overnight. After that, cells were treated with the (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels. After incubation for 48 h, the unused medium was removed, cells washed with PBS, and harvested after trypsinization. In the following, the collected cells were fixed with cold ethanol (70%) for 72 h in the dark condition with a temperature of 4 °C. Eventually, the fixed cells were stained with RibonucleaseA (RnaseA) and PI to analyze by FACScalibur flow cytometer (Becton Dickinson Immunocytometry Systems, San Jose, CA, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the inherent bioimaging potential of the (CDs/DCMC-gel)-FA bionanogels
ce:section.ce:para: The inherent bioimaging potential of the (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels was tested according to the previously published research work [67] with a minor modification. The approach to incubate the cells is as same as the one mentioned in the above cellular tests. In brief, the selected cells were seeded onto sterile coverslips placed in 6-well plates and then incubated overnight at 37 °C. Thereafter, the (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels dispersion in the complete medium was added into the wells and the treated cells were incubated for 0.5 h and 3 h. Last, the cells were washed with PBS and being detected by fluorescence microscopy (Olympus microscope Bh2-RFCA, Japan).

The development of a new nanostructure that could be used as the bioimaging tool and also targeted drug delivery agent is one of the main attractive research areas of biomedicinal research. By considering these, the fluorescent CDs in nanometer size were fabricated via the hydrothermal treatment method using black pepper as a raw material. Black pepper has been used due to its traditional medicinal properties, easy availability, low cost, as well as its capability for obtaining high yield CDs with excellent photostability [56]. Owing to the large surface area, delocalized π electrons, and the presence of a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups on the structure of CDs the drug molecules can efficiently load on CDs through π-π staking, hydrogen bonding, and electrostatic interactions. In the second step, by considering the results of the previously published studies the mixture of a certain amount of CDs and gelatin were chemically crosslinked with DCMC [48,68,69]. DCMC was produced from cellulose as the most abundant polysaccharide [70]. It is a biocompatible, and biodegradable crosslinker that could replace with glutaraldehyde or formaldehyde which relatively are toxic crosslinkers to improve the efficiency of the system [42,43]. So, Jiang et al. evaluated and showed the in vivo biodegradability of DCMC by injection of the DCMC solution into the dorsal skeletal muscle and subcutis [71]. Eventually, the fabricated CDs/DCMC-Gel were conjugated with FA to achieve a targeted drug delivery system for breast cancers; expressing folate receptors on the breast tumor surface. The targeted drug delivery to the tumors could be enhanced using FR-based carriers that can simultaneously incorporate the loaded drug molecules into specific disease sites [72]. The schematic of the (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionangels preparation procedure is illustrated in Fig. 1 . As it is drawn the chemical crosslinking occurred due to the formation of the imine bond as a result of the reaction between the amine functional groups of gelatin with aldehyde functional groups of DCMC. After full characterization with several characterization techniques, the (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionangels were evaluated in the controlled co-delivery of the CUR and DOX. Also, its applicability was analyzed for in vitro bioimaging, as well as its biocompatible and biodegradable nature was validated.

The FT-IR analysis was performed to assess and clarify the DCMC and (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels synthesis, as well as the co-drug loading on (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionangels (Figs. 1S and 2S). Fig. 1S represents the FT-IR spectra of CMC and DCMC. Neat CMC presented the characteristic peaks at 1060 cm−1, 1444 cm−1, 1640 cm−1, 2924 cm−1, and 3500 cm−1 which respectively indicates the stretching vibration of CH-O-CH2, bending vibration of the -OH, stretching vibration of the carboxyl group of CMC, stretching vibration of the -CH2, and stretching vibration of the -OH groups. In comparison with neat CMC, the synthesized DCMC evidenced a new characteristic peak at 1740 cm−1 which can be assigned to the formed CO vibration of the aldehydic carbonyl group as a result of oxidation with sodium periodate. As well as in this spectrum the appeared new peak at 888 cm−1 can be assigned to the formation of the hemiacetal bonds between aldehyde and neighbor hydroxyl groups [73]. The appearance of these two peaks in the FT-IR spectrum of the DCMC can display the successful oxidation of CMC as a result of the reaction with sodium periodate [58]. In the following, the (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels synthesis was evaluated employing this technique. FT-IR spectrum of CDs shows the characteristic peaks related to stretching vibration of the -OH functional groups, CH stretching vibration, asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of the carboxylate ions, and CH out-of-plane bending modes respectively at 3395 cm−1, 2927 cm−1, 1647 cm−1, 1420 cm−1, and 851 cm−1. In addition, this spectrum revealed the peaks at 1156 cm−1 and 1082 cm−1 which were respectively assigned to the CO and CN groups. In conclusion, the appeared peaks suggest that the CDs were fabricated successfully via the hydrothermal treatment of the black pepper. Regarding the pure gelatin, the bands at 1691 cm−1 and 1525 cm−1 respectively are attributed to the CO stretching vibration at amide I and NH bending vibration of amide band II. Besides, the NH stretching vibration, bending vibration of aliphatic CH and CN stretching vibrations were observed at 3374 cm−1, 1458 cm−1, 1334 cm−1, and 1231 cm−1. The FT-IR spectrum of CDs/DCMC-Gel displayed all related peaks of three CDs, gelatin, and DCMC. However, the aldehyde peak of DCMC is not detected in the FT-IR spectrum of CDs/DCMC-Gel which indicates the successful cross-linking reaction between the amino groups of gelatin and aldehyde groups of DCMC. Furthermore, the formed imine bond (-C=N-) as a result of Schiff's base reaction is anticipated to appear at 1625–1645 cm−1 but this peak overlapped with the band related to amide functional groups of gelatin [35]. The observed peak at 1039 cm−1 is related to the presence of CO in the structure of DCMC. All mentioned observations could be the powerful reasons for the successful fabrication of CDs/DCMC-Gel as a result of cross-linking. The common peaks of FA were observed at 1481 cm−1, 1620 cm−1, 1695 cm−1, 2930 cm−1, and 2859 cm−1 which are identified to the stretching vibration of the phenyl ring, CO stretching vibration of amide groups, CO stretching vibration of carboxylic acid, and stretching vibration of CH. Also in this spectrum, the peaks at 3543 cm−1, 3414 cm−1, and 3321 cm−1 assigned to the stretching vibration of hydroxyl and amine functional groups. For the (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels, the observed new peak at 1520–1695 cm−1 recommends the successful functionalization of CDs/DCMC-Gel with FA as a result of the reaction between the remaining amine functional groups of gelatin with carboxylic acid groups of folic acid [70].

Because the interactions among the drug carrier and drugs can be reflected in the characteristic peaks of the FT-IR spectra, in the following, this technique was used to validate the success in CUR and DOX co-loading in (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels. The FT-IR spectrum of neat CUR showed the characteristic absorption bands at 3506 cm−1, 2924 cm−1, 1626 cm−1, 1508 cm−1, 1431 cm−1, and 1276 cm−1 reflecting the stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups of phenolic compounds, -CH groups, carbonyl of enol group, CC of the aromatic ring, bending vibration of assigned alkane CH, stretching vibrations of alcoholic CO. Also, the out-of-plane bending vibration of meta- and para-substituted aromatic systems were recorded as the observed peaks at 857 cm−1 and 812 cm−1. DOX was characterized by the emerging of the absorption band of the primary amide, C-O-C, CO, CH, -OH, and -NH stretching vibration at 889 cm−1 and 776 cm−1, 1117 cm−1, 1724 cm−1, 2920 cm−1, and 3422 cm−1 (as broad peak). The recorded FT-IR spectrum for DOX@CUR@(CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA is the mathematic combination of the DOX, CUR, and (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels FT-IR spectra which correlates the success loading of DOX@CUR on (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of the crystalline structure via XRD analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The crystallinities of the as-synthesized materials; CDs, (CDs/DCMC-Gel), and (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA were studied via XRD and the obtained spectra are given in Fig. 2a. The as-prepared CDs represented a wide peak centered at around 2θ ~18.25° with an interlayer spacing of 0.4858 nm, which is more than graphite interlayer spacing (0.34 nm). The formation of the oxygen-containing groups that were introduced in the synthesis process can be the proof for the observed increase in the interlayer distance and approve the successful synthesis of CDs from black pepper [57]. After encapsulation of the synthesized CDs within the bionanogel structure, a wide peak appeared at 2θ ~14–32° that arose from the successful formation of the gelatin amorph network as a result of crosslinking with DCMC. In the case of (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels the several new sharp diffraction peaks appeared in the XRD spectrum. All new appeared peaks are well-matched with FA and can confirm the successful functionalization of CDs/DCMC-Gel with FA [74]. In addition, one board peak was observed at 2θ ~14–32° which corresponds to the amorph nature of the crosslinked structure.

The successful functionalization of the CDs/DCMC-Gel with FA was further demonstrated by UV–Vis spectroscopy. Fig. 2b shows the recorded UV–Vis spectra for CDs/DCMC-Gel, FA, and (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA after their dispersion in the water. UV–Vis spectrum of CDs/DCMC-Gel revealed two characteristic absorption peaks at 278 nm and 326 nm, respectively demonstrating π–π* electron transition of CC bonds (sp2 domains) and n–π* electron transition of CO bonds (non-bonding oxygen states) [56,57] reported for CDs based materials. On the other hand, FA appeared two key peaks in its UV–Vis spectrum, one main peak at ~280 nm and a shoulder at ~361 nm. Functionalization of CDs/DCMC-Gel with FA obtained the UV–Vis spectrum consisting of the characteristic absorption peaks of both constituents which is a good sign for the success in the modification reaction. The observed slight shift in the wavelength of (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels might be due to performed conjugation reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of CDs and (CDs/DCMC-gel)-FA bionanogels
ce:section.ce:para: PL spectra of the CDs and (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels aqueous dispersion was recorded to investigate and approve their optical performances. Fig. 2c displays the fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs and (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels under an excitation wavelength of 365 nm light at 25 °C. The synthesized CDs presented an emission peak at 426 nm. While the CDs incorporated bionanogels displayed the peak at 438 nm which was shifted slightly. The taken digital photo for the prepared (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels under 365 nm UV light further confirmed the photoluminescent property of (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels, therefore, it is the favorable candidate for biomedical and biological applications [75].

SEM analysis was accomplished to explore the size and morphology of the as-prepared CDs and (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA. The taken images are provided in Fig. 3 . It can be seen that the synthesized CDs have regular spherical morphology with a dimension of ~5–6 nm. Likewise, the CDs/DCMC-Gel (Fig. 3S) and (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA presented the spherical structure. The average size of the fabricated (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels was determined at about 70–90 nm employing the ImageJ software. TEM microscopy was also conducted to further approve the obtained results from the SEM technique related to the shape and dimension. This analysis showed a spherical morphology for (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA which is in line with the obtained results from the SEM [61]. The determined size of the (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA is actually in suitable ranges for each drug delivery system and exhibits its suitability for use as a drug carrier.

The average height profile of the pristine CDs and (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA were evaluated by the AFM analysis. Fig. 4S shows the topology and height profile graphs of each studied sample. As it is reported the bare CDs were evenly dispersed with a thickness of ~3 nm. However, the average height of (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels was ~111 nm. The results of AFM analysis depict the successful preparation of (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA nanogels and are in good accordance with SEM results.

The average hydrodynamic size of the CDs and (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA aqueous suspensions were measured by DLS analysis and the obtained size distribution diagrams are reported in Fig. 5S. The dimension of the synthesized CDs from black pepper was obtained ~13 nm. The average hydrodynamic size increased after the bionanogels formation; reached 322 nm. The observed increase in the particle size after the binanogels formation could be due to the formed crosslinked network and encapsulation of the CDs within it. Comparing the results achieved a larger particle size from DLS in comparison with SEM which is poison to the fact that the DLS measurement is occurred in the aqueous environment, while SEM analysis performs on the collapse forms of them.

Swelling is one of the main factors that can affect the drug load and release behavior of each nanogel. Therefore, the swelling of the prepared system was explored at buffer solution with two different pHs; pH 7.4 and 5.0 which simulate the pH of the physiological and tumor cells conditions. Fig. 4a shows the water uptake percentage of (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels at 37 °C. As it is reported in Fig. 4a an increase of pH resulted in a decrease in the swelling percentage. The equilibrium swelling of the (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels at pH 5.0 was 605.2% while at pH 7.4 was 323.01%. This observation is due to the expansion of the (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels network at acidic media owing to protonation of the amine groups of gelatin. So, as a result of formed electrostatic repulsion and the expansion of the nanogel network a high amount of water molecules can enter into the (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels structure, and the swelling ratio increase.

In the anticancer drug delivery area, it is essential to develop novel biocompatible nanocarriers with satisfactory colloidal stability. Therefore the colloidal stability of the synthesized carrier was evaluated in water, PBS, FBS, and complete medium (RPMI containing 10% of FBS). As shown in Fig. 4b, in all of the four studied conditions the (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels maintained colloidal stability up to 3 days of incubation, suggesting their outstanding stability under the biological condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro biodegradation study of (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels
ce:section.ce:para: Biodegradable systems are expected to degrade when exposed to bioactive agents, this feature makes them a favorable candidate for diverse biomedical applications. The biodegradability of CDs [76] and DCMC [71] were approved in the published works. Therefore, herein the biodegradability of the (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels as a newly synthesized system was investigated in the presence of trypsin. It was detected that the weight of the CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA nanogels decreased with time. So, during the 72 h and at a temperature of 37 °C about 51% of CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels were degraded in the presence of trypsin. The obtained degradation data for the CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels in the presence of trypsin displayed that the CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA has degradable nature. These results are in agreement with the fact that trypsin appears to be the most suitable enzyme for the degradation of gelatin-based systems [77].

The samples with antioxidant properties have wide-ranging applications in biological systems because in these systems the free radicals are quenched by the antioxidant. DPPH radical scavenging activity of CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels was investigated for 30 min and the resulting UV–Vis spectra are illustrated in Fig. 5a. CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels displayed the DPPH radical scavenging effect; 25% which approves its antioxidant efficiency that may be related to the presence of antioxidant CDs as well as FA in the structure. These results further confirmed their usability in many cancer treatment applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DOX and CUR co-load and release
ce:section.ce:para: CUR and DOX are two examples of the commonly used anticancer drugs with a wide range of antitumor activities. However, their direct administration is restricted owing to their critical side effects. Therefore, today's numerous scientific researches are focused on obtaining the appropriate strategies for improving the therapeutic potential of anticancer drugs. In this regard, nanogels are more favorable because of their special structural properties. By considering these, here, we designed, prepared, and investigated the in vitro drug delivery capability of (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels for both CUR and DOX delivery. The entrapment efficiency of CUR and DOX in (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA respectively were about 44% and 41.4%, also the loading capacity of CUR and DOX were obtained 7.33% and 6.9%. The formation of the electrostatic interactions as well as the hydrogen bonds between the polar functional group of drug molecules with these moieties in (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels are two possible participated factors in observed drug loading values. Moreover, the hydrophobic interactions of CUR with the CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA and also the π–π interaction between CUR or DOX with the aromatic region of (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA could be other reasons [53,69].

In the continue to simulate the intracellular drug release conditions and validate the effect of intrinsic stimuli pH, the drug release behavior was examined at 37 °C and two different pHs; pH 7.4 and pH 5.0 which respectively simulate the pH of the physiological and tumor conditions. As it is depicted in Fig. 5b, the cumulative release percentage of both drugs from DOX@CUR@(CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA was decreased as a result of pH increasing. DOX@CUR@(CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA released about 57.10% of DOX and 48.79% of CUR respectively at pH 5.0 after 72 h. On the contrary, this system only released about 31.73% of DOX and 25.57% of CUR at pH 7.4 in a comparable period of time. As the microenvironment of cancer cells is commonly acidic, this controlled release of both drug molecules in the simulated endosomal environment specifies the potential of DOX@CUR@(CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA for breast cancer therapy. Several factors can participate in the observed drug release profile and release percentage that the rupture of the (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA nanogels as breaking of the formed imine bonds bring the more drug release at the acidic condition and is the most important and main effective reason [40]. Moreover, the weakening of the hydrogen bonds and π–π stacking interactions between drug molecules and (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels at acidic conditions are the other factors. Finally, the protonation of the remaining amine functional group of the gelatin leads to the extension of the bionanogels network, more buffer penetration, and high drug releases at pH 5.0. As a result, because the microenvironment of tumor cells is usually acidic, both DOX and CUR would come out inside the tumor cells in a controlled manner, which causes an increase in the bioavailability to the cancer cells.

Both CUR and DOX release data were fitted with Eq. (8)–(11) to elucidate the mechanism that is well fit with experimental release data. The achieved results are listed in Figs. 6 and 7
, and Table 1 . As it is reported for both drugs at (pH 7.4, 37 °C) and (pH 5.0, 37 °C) respectively the Korsmeyer-Peppas and Higuchi model with the highest correlation coefficient (R2) are the best fitting with experimental results. In the following, the obtained “n” was utilized to more specify the drug diffusion mechanism. The “n” parameter for all tested conditions was obtained about 0.45–1.0. Considering the calculated values the drug release follows the non-Fickian mechanism in both studied conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cell viability determination via MTT assay
ce:section.ce:para: In the current work, CDs and DCMC were used respectively as the biocompatible fluorescent NPs and crosslinkers. The biocompatibility of the CDs [56] and DCMC [71,78] previously was approved in the published works; cell viability higher than 75%. So, the cited references displayed that there was no significant growth inhibition to non-cancerous cells for both CDs and DCMC. Therefore in the current research, the MDA-MB 231 cell viability rate was explored to study the in vitro biocompatibility of the newly synthesized (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels and their antitumor effect after drug loading. The measured viability for the treated cells with different concentrations of the studied formulations is shown in Fig. 8a and b. Moreover, the biocompatibility of the (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels was investigated toward MCF 10A as a model of normal cells (Fig. 6S). Results exhibit that the (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels are biocompatible even after treatment in high concentrations. All studied samples exhibited the dose-dependent reduction in cell viability and cell viability was decreased by increasing the sample concentration. As it is presented in Fig. 8a and Fig. 6S, no noticeable evidence of cytotoxicity was detected for both studied cell lines after treatment with various concentrations of (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels, signifying cytocompatibility of it for administring as a drug carrier [4]. However, over time, free DOX@CUR and DOX@CUR@(CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA showed antitumor effects on the MDA-MB 231 breast cancer cell line, and the rate of cell killing for DOX@CUR@(CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA was higher than free DOX@CUR. The involving the distinct pathways are in drug molecules delivering to the cancer cells may be the reason for this observation. In conclusion, the obtained cell viability rates indicate the feasibility of (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA using as a biocompatible and targeted carrier for DOX@CUR delivery with higher efficiency.

In vitro cellular uptake study was performed to evaluate and compare the efficiency of DOX@CUR, DOX@CUR@(CDs/DCMC-Gel), and DOX@CUR@(CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA for uptaking in MDA-MB 231 cells (Fig. 9 ). In this regard, the cells were treated with each of the samples and then incubated. After 3 h the treated cells were observed and then imaged by microscope. The strong red fluorescence of DOX and green fluorescence of CUR was observed for the treated cells with all of the samples, indicating their ability for internalization into cells. The fluorescence intensity for the treated cells with both DOX@CUR@(CDs/DCMC-Gel) and DOX@CUR@(CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA was higher than that of DOX@CUR which is in good accordance with MTT results. This observation could be related to the positive role of the synthesized bionanogels in drug carrying to the cells in high efficiency. Moreover, at the same incubation time, it is detectable that the fluorescence intensity for the treated cells with DOX@CUR@(CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA relatively is more than DOX@CUR@(CDs/DCMC-Gel) that could be related to the FA-induced targeting for breast cancer treatment as a result of its taken up by cancer cells via folate receptor (glycophosphatidylinositol FRs) mediated endocytosis. Overall, the fluorescence microscopy results showed the targeting effect of FA conjugated nanogels [79,80].

ce:section.ce:section-title: DAPI staining analysis for morphological changes of the nucleus
ce:section.ce:para: The presence of the fragmented and condensed DNA in the apoptotic cells was investigated employing fluorescence microscopic analysis of DAPI stained MDA-MB 231 cells. The taken microscopic images of the DAPI-stained cells after 48 h exposure to (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA, DOX@CUR, and DOX@CUR@(CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA are shown in Fig. 10 . The biocompotibility of (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionnogels on the cells was further approved by DAPI staining, so the nucleus of the treated cells with(CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionnogels remained intact. However, the typical morphological markers of apoptosis-like nuclear shrinkage, condensation, and fragmentation or remodeling in the chromatin and DNA rings were observed for the treated cells with DOX@CUR, and DOX@CUR@(CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA. Additionally, the morphological changes in the nucleus of treated cells with DOX@CUR@(CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA were higher than free DOX@CUR which further approve its efficiency in cancer treatment [4].

Cell cycle analysis has been explored by flow cytometry using PI staining to more explore the biocompatibility of (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels through their effect on the normal cycle of the selected cell line (Fig. 11a). The area under the peak was calculated to obtain the phase within the cell cycle. Compared with control, the cell cycle profile of the (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA did not show a notable effect on cell cycle progression that certificate its biocompatibility. This result verifies the cell viability data and establishes that the (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA is a biocompatible drug carrier.

The usefulness of (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels as a bioimaging vehicle was evaluated by using a microscope. Fig. 11b displays the microscopic images of MDA-MB 231 cells incubated with (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA for 0.5 h and 3 h. As it is detectable the incubated MDA-MB 231 cells with (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA displayed strong blue-color fluorescence, indicating good cellular uptake of (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels. Moreover, this finding support that the (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels have the potential to be used as a bio-imaging probe, besides its capability in the treatment of breast cancer.

Briefly in this article, the focus was to develop and evaluate the targeted dual drug delivery potential of the produced photoluminescent (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels for breast cancer therapeutics. The prepared (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels were characterized by several characterization techniques. DLS obtained the hydrodynamic diameter of (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels about 322 nm. The prepared system was investigated for co-delivery of CUR and DOX. A pH-sensitive drug release behavior was observed for DOX@CUR@(CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA. DPPH test confirmed the radical scavenging capability of (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA; 25% of antioxidant activity was observed. The MTT and cell cycle test results exhibited that the (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels are biocompatible. But DOX@CUR@(CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA showed enhanced cytotoxicity on the 48 h in MDA-MB 231 cells. Moreover, the cell uptake test revealed its higher efficiency in cancer cells uptaking. Given this opportunity, the (CDs/DCMC-Gel)-FA bionanogels could be proposed as an ideal candidate for the targeted co-delivery of anticancer drugs with inherent bioimaging potential.

Malihe Pooresmaeil carried out the experiment. Malihe Pooresmaeil wrote the manuscript with support from Hassan Namazi as a supervisor of the project. All authors discussed the results and contributed to the final manuscript.
